perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees not to disclose or reveal any Confidential Information , including the existence of the Listing, to any persons or entities other than Buyer’s employees or representatives who are directly participating in the evaluation of th e information , and only then in connection with the proposed acquisition.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees not to disclose or reveal any Confidential Information , including the existence of the Listing, to any persons or entities other than Buyer’s representatives who are directly participating in the evaluation of th e information , and only then in connection with the proposed acquisition.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to 'Buyer's employees' and only allowing disclosure to 'Buyer's representatives,' the contract restricts the scope of permissible disclosure more than is generally allowed under trade secret law, creating ambiguity and potential conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11 allows for recovery of damages for unfair or deceptive trade practices, which could include overly restrictive non-disclosure agreements that hinder legitimate business operations. By unduly restricting the flow of information to employees within the buyer's organization, this clause could potentially be viewed as an unfair business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

perturbation.justification: The changed text restricts the disclosure of confidential information to 'Buyer's representatives' only, removing 'Buyer's employees' from the permissible recipients. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this creates ambiguity and potential conflict with trade secret law. The Law Explanation points to M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, arguing that overly restrictive non-disclosure agreements could be seen as unfair trade practices. The scraped snippets, both referencing Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93a, Section 11, allow for actions against unfair trade practices. By limiting disclosure more than is arguably necessary for legitimate business operations, the changed text creates a risk of being deemed an unfair practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 11, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: All correspondence, inquiries , offers to purchase and negotiations relating to the purchase or lease of any Listing presented by Broker will be conducted exclusively through Broker.
perturbation.changed_text: All inquiries and negotiations relating to the purchase of any Listing presented by Broker will be conducted exclusively through Broker. Offers to purchase can be delivered by any means to the Listing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the buyer to directly submit offers, bypassing the broker. This creates conflict if the listing agreement mandates all offers to go through the broker, which is common.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. If the broker's listing agreement requires all offers to be presented through them, directly circumventing the broker could be deemed an unfair business practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified text allows offers to be submitted directly to the Listing Party, bypassing the broker. This contradicts M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices. If the original agreement requires all offers to go through the broker, circumventing them could be deemed an unfair business practice as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state that 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' By allowing offers to bypass the broker, the changed text creates a situation where the broker's role could be undermined, potentially leading to an unfair practice.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer understands that if Buyer interferes with Broker’s contract with any Listing Party , Buyer will be personally liable to Broker for the payment of Broker’s commission.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer understands that if Buyer directly voids Broker’s contract with any Listing Party , Buyer will be liable to Broker for a percentage of Broker’s commission.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'interferes' to 'directly voids' narrows the scope of prohibited actions, and changing 'the payment' to 'a percentage' introduces ambiguity regarding the amount owed. The modification makes the penalty less certain.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 259, § 7 (Statute of Frauds) requires certain agreements, including those regarding real estate commissions, to be in writing and clearly defined. Changing the liability to a 'percentage' of the commission introduces ambiguity and could render the agreement unenforceable if the percentage is not explicitly defined in writing.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts M.G.L. c. 259, § 7 (Statute of Frauds) because it introduces ambiguity regarding the broker's commission. The original text specifies 'the payment of Broker’s commission,' implying a definite amount. The changed text alters this to 'a percentage of Broker’s commission,' which is ambiguous if the percentage is not clearly defined. This ambiguity could make the agreement unenforceable, as it doesn't clearly define the amount owed. The Law Explanation confirms that the Statute of Frauds requires agreements regarding real estate commissions to be clearly defined in writing, and the change introduces ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: All information regarding the Listing is provided by the Listing Party or other sources and is deemed reliable, but not verified in any way by Broker. Broker makes no warranty, expressed or implied, as to the accuracy of such information.
perturbation.changed_text: All information regarding the Listing is provided by the Listing Party or other sources and is deemed reliable, but not verified in any way by Broker. Broker's duty of care is limited to forwarding the information. Buyer agrees that Broker is not responsible for the accuracy or completeness of any of the information Buyer receives or fails to receive, and B uyer agrees to, defend , indemnify and hold harmless Broker and any of its agents from any claims or damages which may occur by reason of the inaccuracy or incompleteness of any information provided to Buyer with respect to any business Buyer might purchase .
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'Broker's duty of care is limited to forwarding the information' could be interpreted as attempting to waive liability for negligent misrepresentation, which is limited in Massachusetts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While brokers can disclaim warranties, attempting to completely waive responsibility for negligent misrepresentation could be deemed an unfair or deceptive practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 if the broker knew or should have known the information was false or misleading and failed to exercise reasonable care in forwarding it.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause limiting the broker's duty of care to merely forwarding information and includes an indemnification clause, potentially waiving liability for negligent misrepresentation. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts if the broker knew or should have known the information was false or misleading. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, referencing M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2(a), declare unlawful "unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce." By attempting to limit liability for inaccurate information, the modified text contradicts this law, as it could be interpreted as an attempt to engage in a deceptive practice by potentially shielding the broker from responsibility for negligently providing false information.
